snapshot_html: The base case is still de-escalation, but the picture turned two-sided overnight. Israeli strikes on Hezbollah in Lebanon — which have killed dozens despite a renewed ceasefire — postponed the US–Iran talks in Switzerland (VP Vance cancelled his trip), and Tehran says it won't treat the next negotiating phase as started until Lebanon is resolved. Rhetoric hardened: Iran's military command threatened to close the Strait of Hormuz over Lebanon, while President Trump warned he could "hit Iran very hard again." Even so, markets keep pricing the peace path: Brent slid to ~$73 (lowest since late February) as Hormuz tanker traffic actually increased, and gold sank to ~$3,981, an eight-month low, under a hawkish Fed and a dollar at multi-year highs (DXY ~101.4). Equities were only modestly soft — S&P ~7,358 (−0.1%), Nikkei ~69,175 (−0.9%) — while the Hang Seng edged up to ~23,420 and USD/JPY held firm near ~160. Asia-Pacific stays a slow-burn second front (China Coast Guard pressure near Taiwan; the China–Japan freeze). Net: still the de-escalation playbook, but keep tail-hedges live while the talks are stalled and the Hormuz threat is back on the table.

scenarios.name: Truce holds / talks resume
scenarios.desc: Lebanon flare-up cools, Switzerland talks restart, the 60-day MOU path holds and Hormuz stays open. Residual war premium keeps bleeding out.

scenarios.name: Frozen conflict / simmering status quo
scenarios.desc: Lebanon keeps the talks stalled; no permanent deal, periodic flare-ups and Hormuz threats. Some premium returns and stays sticky.

scenarios.name: Re-escalation / wider war
scenarios.desc: Israel-Lebanon spirals, Iran walks from the MOU, Hormuz threatened or hit, US drawn deeper in after Trump's warning.

scenarios.name: Asia-Pacific second front opens
scenarios.desc: China pressure on Taiwan/Japan turns kinetic while the Middle East is hot. Lower odds near-term but highest impact.

track.note: Fade-oil call kept delivering as Brent fell to ~$73 on rising Hormuz traffic, but stalled US-Iran talks and Iran's fresh Hormuz threat tilt the regime to mixed — keep fading rallies while respecting genuine two-way risk.
